executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

**Enrollment and Spending Trends**

National Medicaid enrollment reached 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with most major states experiencing enrollment declines following the end of pandemic-related continuous enrollment protections. The pattern is consistent across large states, with California leading declines at -6.2% year-over-year, followed by Michigan at -7.6% and Illinois at -6.2%. Texas experienced a more moderate -3.1% decline, aligning closely with Pennsylvania's reduction rate. Only North Carolina bucked the trend with slight growth of +0.5%. Total program expenditures reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with significant variation in per-enrollee costs across states. Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee, positioning it near the national average of $2,869, while states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Massachusetts ($4,201) demonstrate substantially higher per-capita investments. Florida represents the most efficient large state at $2,031 per enrollee, though this may reflect different service delivery models or beneficiary acuity levels.

**Quality Performance and State Comparisons**

Quality measure performance reveals substantial interstate variation, with the national average score of 65.6 out of 100 indicating significant room for improvement across the program. Texas scored 62 points, ranking 30th nationally in the "moderate" performance tier, trailing behind leading states like Massachusetts (81), Minnesota (80), and Vermont (79) by nearly 20 points. This performance gap suggests potential opportunities for Texas to adopt best practices from high-performing states, particularly given its high managed care penetration rate of 93.3%—well above the national average of 83.2%. The concentration of low-performing states in the Southeast, with Mississippi (48), Alabama (52), and South Carolina (53) anchoring the bottom tier, indicates regional challenges that may warrant targeted federal technical assistance and policy interventions to address underlying structural barriers to quality improvement.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing roughly 5.4% of national enrollment despite having 9% of the U.S. population—a reflection of the state's decision not to expand Medicaid under the ACA. The program's enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, aligning closely with the national deceleration trend as pandemic-era continuous coverage protections unwind. At $2,988 per enrollee, Texas spends 4.2% above the national average of $2,869, positioning it in the middle range among large states—significantly below high-cost states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and New York ($3,431), but above Florida's notably lean $2,031 per enrollee. Texas demonstrates operational strength in managed care deployment, with 93.3% penetration rate exceeding the national average of 83.2%, indicating sophisticated program administration and risk-based care coordination.

However, quality outcomes present Texas's most significant challenge. The state's quality score of 62/100 ranks 30th nationally and falls below the national average of 65.6, clustering Texas among moderate performers rather than the high-achieving cohort. This is particularly concerning when compared to peer non-expansion states like North Carolina, which has achieved better cost efficiency ($2,616 per enrollee) while maintaining quality parity. Among southern states, Texas lags behind North Carolina and Virginia in quality metrics, though it outperforms lower-tier states like Arkansas (54) and Alabama (52). The combination of higher-than-average per-enrollee costs with below-average quality outcomes suggests potential inefficiencies in care delivery that warrant attention. Key areas to monitor include whether Texas's strong managed care infrastructure can drive quality improvements, how the state manages ongoing enrollment transitions post-PHE, and whether procurement activities signal investments in program modernization or quality enhancement initiatives.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost efficiency issues.

alerts.title: Michigan Medicaid Enrollment Drops 7.6% Year-Over-Year
alerts.description: Michigan experienced the largest enrollment decline at -7.6%, well above the 10% threshold for concerning enrollment changes.

alerts.title: Five States Score Below 55 on Quality Measures
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi have critically low quality scores, with Mississippi at only 48/100.

alerts.title: Florida Low Per-Enrollee Spending Despite Large Population
alerts.description: Florida spends only $2,031 per enrollee, 29% below the national average, which may indicate either efficiency or potential underfunding.

alerts.title: High Spending States Show Mixed Quality Performance
alerts.description: Pennsylvania and Ohio have high per-enrollee costs ($4,377 and $3,561) but their quality scores are not available for correlation analysis.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score at risk tier with 20-point gap to top performers
executiveInsights.summary: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a quality score of 62/100, sitting just above the low performer threshold (<60) and 19 points below the high performer benchmark (≥75). This places Texas in the bottom 60% nationally despite having the 3rd largest Medicaid program.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts federal funding, regulatory scrutiny, and contract renewals. Being in the moderate tier with such a large program creates significant financial and reputational risk for both HHSC and Accenture.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement as immediate priority in next client meeting

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs 4% above national average despite high MC penetration
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, despite having 93.3% managed care penetration (10 points above national average of 83.2%). This suggests managed care efficiency gains are not materializing as expected.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration should drive lower per-enrollee costs, but Texas is underperforming. This indicates potential managed care contract optimization opportunities worth hundreds of millions given the $12B total spend.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care contract performance and cost benchmarking analysis

executiveInsights.title: New HCBS quality measures for 2028 announced in Federal Register
executiveInsights.summary: CMS published new Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set for 2028, indicating upcoming regulatory changes that will impact quality scoring and potentially funding.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Given Texas's moderate quality performance, new HCBS measures could significantly impact future scores and federal funding. Early preparation and system updates will be critical for compliance and performance improvement.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review new HCBS quality requirements and assess Texas readiness gaps

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment decline of 3.1% impacts $12B program revenue base
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% year-over-year to 4,032,864 enrollees, representing a loss of approximately 129,000 members. This decline affects the revenue base for the $12.048B program.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines directly impact program funding and may signal unwinding effects or eligibility changes. This trend could affect contract scope, staffing needs, and revenue projections for Accenture's services.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag enrollment trend analysis for next quarterly business review

executiveInsights.title: Active HHS OIG oversight creates compliance monitoring requirements
executiveInsights.summary: HHS OIG maintains active Medicaid portfolio oversight and work plan items, indicating ongoing federal scrutiny of state Medicaid programs including potential Texas reviews.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: OIG oversight can result in audit findings, corrective action plans, and financial recoveries. Proactive compliance monitoring and documentation becomes critical for risk mitigation on the Texas contract.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review current OIG work plan items for Texas-specific risks

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th with 62/100 quality score, creating vulnerability to federal oversight and potential funding penalties.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement roadmap targeting top 3 performance gaps.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY with 4M+ enrollees, indicating potential unwinding impacts or eligibility tightening.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position analytics capabilities to help HHSC optimize enrollment retention strategies.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality expansion
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 HCBS quality measure set creates compliance requirements and modernization opportunities for community-based services.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop HCBS quality measurement and reporting solution for Texas implementation.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: Texas has 93.3% MC penetration with 3.8M+ enrollees, suggesting upcoming contract renewals worth $12B+ annually.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor HHSC procurement portal for managed care RFPs and prepare competitive positioning.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio oversight creates audit risk but also opportunities for compliance consulting.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring capabilities and proactive audit preparation services.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee versus national average of $2,869, indicating moderate efficiency with optimization potential.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose cost management analytics to identify high-impact savings opportunities.

riskOpportunity.label: CHIP data collection changes
riskOpportunity.description: New Medicaid and CHIP information collection activities require system updates and reporting enhancements.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop automated reporting solutions for new CHIP data collection requirements.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive displacement risk
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple states show procurement activity through ESBD and other portals, indicating active market competition for Texas contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Intensify relationship management with key HHSC stakeholders and differentiate service offerings.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid serves 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, better than many large states.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee annually in fiscal year 2024.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees in managed care.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas quality score is 62 out of 100, ranking 30th nationally in moderate tier.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active Texas procurement portals are being monitored for Medicaid opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Three active OIG oversight items and recent Medicaid regulatory activities detected.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific upcoming milestones identified in the available data.
